terms.definition: The degree to which the parts of a complex system remain in constructive alignment, enabling reliable prediction and joint action
A semi-autonomous functional unit within the larger social system (e.g., family, judiciary)
A stable pattern of reciprocal expectations among agents that lowers transaction costs
The validated transmission of signals that update agents’ world-models
Loss of alignment such that coordination benefits drop to zero; the opposite pole of coherence
A scalar metric in the closed interval [0,1] summarizing whole-system coherence across social subsystems
Empirically observed χ value below which spontaneous recovery has not been documented in historical cases (~0.35)
A polity maintaining low overt crime primarily via ubiquitous monitoring and punitive feedback loops
terms.first_use_section: Why Coherence Matters More Than Wealth

terms.definition: An abrupt qualitative change in system state as a control parameter crosses a critical point
